text:
CT SCAN OF THE CHEST, ABDOMEN, AND PELVIS WITH INTRAVENOUS CONTRAST

CLINICAL INDICATION: Marcus T. Delgado, 59-year-old male with known stage IV sigmoid colon adenocarcinoma with hepatic and pulmonary metastases, currently on third-line TAS-102/Avastin (cycle 5). Restaging evaluation. Known KRAS G12D mutation. Prior imaging demonstrating multiple bilateral pulmonary nodules and multifocal bilobar hepatic lesions. Rising CEA trend. Evaluate for interval change.

TECHNIQUE: Multidetector CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis performed with intravenous iohexol contrast (100 mL, 3 mL/sec), with acquisitions in portal venous phase for the abdomen and pelvis and standard contrast-enhanced technique for the chest. Axial, coronal, and sagittal reformatted images reviewed. Comparison made to prior CT CAP from approximately 3 months prior.

---

FINDINGS:

CHEST:

Lungs and Airways: There has been clear and substantial interval progression of bilateral pulmonary metastatic disease compared to the prior examination. Six pulmonary nodules are now identified in total, representing an increase from five on the preceding scan. The right lower lobe nodule, previously the dominant lesion at 1.1-1.2 cm, has increased to 1.8 cm and now demonstrates a more irregular, spiculated margin compared with its prior smooth configuration — morphologically consistent with progressive metastatic involvement. A right upper lobe nodule has increased from 0.7 cm to 1.2 cm, and a right middle lobe nodule has increased from 0.6 cm to 0.9 cm. On the left, a left lower lobe nodule now measures 1.3 cm (previously 0.8 cm), the lingular nodule has grown from 0.5 cm to 0.8 cm, and a newly identified left lower lobe nodule measuring 0.7 cm is present posteriorly that was not appreciated on the prior study. No nodule demonstrates calcification or fat density to suggest a benign etiology. No cavitation. No endobronchial lesion. The major airways are patent. Mild peribronchial thickening is noted in the right lower lobe, possibly reflecting peribronchovascular lymphangitic spread versus reactive change — this is a new finding and warrants attention on follow-up. No consolidation or pneumonia.

Pleura: A small right-sided pleural effusion is now present, measuring approximately 5-6 mm in maximal depth posteriorly, new from the prior examination. No left pleural effusion. No pneumothorax. No pleural nodularity identified.

Mediastinum and Hila: The mediastinum is midline. A small right paratracheal lymph node measures 0.9 cm in short axis — this is enlarged compared to 0.5 cm on the prior study and raises concern for early metastatic mediastinal nodal involvement, though it falls at the borderline of pathologic size criteria. Right hilar fullness is noted. Mild cardiomegaly is present, unchanged from prior. No pericardial effusion. The thoracic aorta is of normal caliber.

Chest Wall and Bones of the Thorax: No lytic or sclerotic osseous lesions identified. No rib fractures. Mild multilevel degenerative changes of the thoracic spine.

---

ABDOMEN:

Liver: There has been marked and diffuse interval worsening of the hepatic metastatic burden, representing the most striking finding on this examination. The previously dominant segment VI lesion, which measured 2.9 cm on the prior study, has grown substantially and now measures 4.2 cm — a nearly 50% increase in diameter over approximately three months. This lesion demonstrates peripheral rim enhancement with central hypoenhancement and internal heterogeneity, suggesting central necrosis within a large and rapidly growing deposit. The previously documented segment V lesion has also increased, now measuring 1.4 cm (from 0.9 cm). The previously stable segment IVa lesion has grown from 1.1 cm to 1.8 cm, and the segment II lesion has increased from 0.7 cm to 1.2 cm.

In addition to growth in the previously characterized lesions, at least two new hepatic lesions have been identified that were not present or measurable on the prior study: a lesion in segment III measuring approximately 1.4 cm, and a lesion in segment VIII measuring approximately 1.1 cm. Both demonstrate peripheral arterial phase enhancement and washout appearance on portal venous phase imaging, highly characteristic of metastatic deposits in this clinical context. A third subcentimeter new lesion in segment VII (0.7 cm) is suspicious but not definitively characterized on this single study.

Overall, the hepatic parenchymal involvement has substantially increased, with metastatic deposits now distributed across segments II, III, IVa, V, VI, VII, and VIII — representing nearly diffuse bilobar involvement. The total hepatic metastatic burden appears significantly greater than on any prior study. The background liver parenchyma demonstrates mild heterogeneity. No biliary ductal dilatation is identified. The portal vein and hepatic veins are patent on this examination, though the segment VI lesion abuts the right hepatic vein without definite occlusion.

Spleen: Normal in size at 11.2 cm. No splenic lesions.

Pancreas: The pancreas is normal in size and enhancement. No ductal dilatation. No peripancreatic stranding.

Kidneys and Adrenal Glands: Both kidneys enhance symmetrically. No hydronephrosis. No nephrolithiasis. Simple renal cysts bilaterally. The adrenal glands are normal in morphology without discrete nodules.

Gastrointestinal Tract and Sigmoid Colon Primary: The primary sigmoid colon mass is again identified. It appears as a focal area of wall thickening and luminal narrowing in the sigmoid colon, measuring approximately 3.6 cm in the craniocaudal dimension on axial and coronal reconstructions — slightly increased from 3.4 cm on the prior scan. There is pericolonic fat stranding surrounding the mass. No upstream colonic obstruction or luminal dilation. The remainder of the colon is decompressed and of normal caliber. No pneumoperitoneum. Small bowel loops are normal in caliber and distribution.

Peritoneum and Ascites: A new finding of small but definite free fluid in the pelvis and perihepatic space is identified on this examination, measuring up to approximately 1-2 cm in depth in the most dependent portions of the pelvis (pouch of Douglas) and a thin rim of perihepatic fluid. This represents new small-volume ascites compared to the prior study on which no free fluid was present. No discrete peritoneal nodules or omental caking are identified on this examination, though the presence of ascites in this clinical context is concerning for early peritoneal involvement that may not yet be nodularly apparent. The ascites appears simple/serous in density.

Retroperitoneum and Lymph Nodes: Multiple retroperitoneal and mesenteric lymph nodes are again noted, the largest being a mesenteric nodal conglomerate measuring approximately 2.0 x 1.5 cm, mildly increased from prior. Aortocaval lymph nodes are borderline in size. No discrete para-aortic lymphadenopathy exceeding 1 cm in short axis is definitively new, though overall nodal burden appears mildly increased.

Blood Vessels: The abdominal aorta is of normal caliber without aneurysmal dilation. The celiac axis, superior mesenteric artery, and inferior mesenteric artery are patent. No thrombosis.

---

PELVIS:

The urinary bladder is adequately distended and smooth-walled. The prostate gland is mildly enlarged at approximately 35 cc, unchanged. No pelvic sidewall adenopathy meeting definitive pathologic size criteria. Pelvic floor and musculature are unremarkable. The previously noted free fluid in the pouch of Douglas is detailed above.

---

BONES:

No lytic or blastic osseous lesions to suggest skeletal metastases. Mild lumbar spondylosis. No pathologic fractures. The sacrum and iliac bones are unremarkable.

---

IMPRESSION:

1. Substantial and diffuse interval progression of hepatic metastatic disease compared to the prior examination approximately three months ago. The segment VI lesion, now 4.2 cm, has grown markedly with evidence of internal necrosis. At least two to three new hepatic lesions are identified (segments III, VIII, and a subcentimeter lesion in segment VII), with growth in all previously documented lesions. The overall hepatic metastatic burden is now extensive with involvement spanning nearly all hepatic segments, representing a significant and clinically important change from prior.

2. Progression of bilateral pulmonary metastatic disease, with growth in all previously identified nodules and identification of at least one new left lower lobe nodule. The right lower lobe dominant nodule has grown to 1.8 cm and developed irregular margins. Six pulmonary nodules now identified in total.

3. New mild right pleural effusion, new from prior.

4. New small-volume pelvic and perihepatic ascites, new from prior, raising concern for early peritoneal dissemination in the appropriate clinical context, though no discrete peritoneal deposits are identified.

5. A mildly enlarged right paratracheal lymph node (0.9 cm), borderline and increased from prior, indeterminate — follow-up or further evaluation as clinically warranted.

6. Mild interval increase in size of the primary sigmoid colon mass (3.6 cm), without evidence of obstruction.

7. No brain imaging obtained on this study; dedicated brain imaging may be considered given overall disease trajectory if clinically indicated.

Overall, this scan documents clear and multi-compartment disease progression across hepatic, pulmonary, and likely peritoneal sites. The pace of progression and the extent of new hepatic involvement are notably greater than seen on prior studies, suggesting accelerating disease biology despite systemic therapy. These findings represent a significant clinical watershed event.
